retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Standardized Lateral Cephalometric Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral skull radiographs provide a 2-dimensional representation of the head and neck and measure the sagittal and vertical dimensions of the skull. Sagittal measurements assess the position and inclination of the maxilla and mandible, while vertical measurements evaluate the height of the facial structures and the relationship between the jaw. [4] In contrast, posteroanterior radiographs, taken from the front of the head, measure transverse and vertical dimensions, offering insights into the width of the face and the relationship between the jaws in the transverse plane. [5] However, in clinical practice, cephalometric analysis primarily relies on lateral radiographs, as posteroanterior projections are more challenging to interpret.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Standardized Lateral Cephalometric Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: A standardized technique is essential for obtaining radiographs to ensure accurate comparisons over time and between patients. Image quality depends heavily on the proper positioning of the patient. The patient should be positioned with the Frankfort plane horizontal, ear rests placed in the external auditory meatuses, the nasion aligned on the bridge of the nose, and teeth in centric occlusion. [6] The radiographic source is fixed at a distance of 5 feet (150–180 cm) from the patient's mid-sagittal plane, with a film-to-mid-sagittal plane distance of 30 cm. [6] A calibrated steel ruler is included in each image, ensuring precise measurements are documented.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Selection of Crown
retrieved_docs.content: The operator can select the crown using different methods: Measuring the mesiodistal dimension of the tooth before tooth preparation Trial and error method Crown selection after tooth preparation

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- History and Physical -- Dental anterior crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: In dental anterior crossbite, 1 or more teeth are involved. The profile is straight in centric occlusion and centric relation. Class I molar and canine relation can be seen. SNA, SNB, and ANB angles are within normal limits. It can be due to abnormal axial dental inclination.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Anteroposterior Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An increased SNA angle indicates that the maxilla is positioned protrusively relative to the cranial base compared to the average. [3] Conversely, a decreased SNA angle suggests that the maxilla is retruded relative to the cranial base compared to the norm. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: 3-3-2 Rule -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: 3: A measurement of 3 fingers from the anterior tip of the mandible to the anterior neck estimates the volume of the submandibular space. In a typical patient, it is possible to place 3 fingers on the floor of the mandible between the mental angle and the neck in the vicinity of the hyoid bone. Under normal circumstances, this distance should measure approximately 7 cm. If the measurement is less than 3 finger widths, it suggests that the laryngeal axis is more acute than the pharyngeal axis. This indicates that aligning the oral opening to the pharyngeal opening will be challenging. Furthermore, it suggests that there will be limited space available for the displacement of the tongue within the throat. The 3-finger rule has certain limitations, as the distance can vary based on factors such as height and ethnicity. To address these limitations, an alternative method known as the ratio of height-to-thyromental distance (RHTMD) has been proposed.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Occlusal Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Before placing the rubber dam and preparing the tooth, observe the following: Whether the opposing tooth has extruded as a consequence of long-standing carious lesions Whether there is mesial drift as a result of carious lesions leading to proximal space loss Presence of spacing and crowding Examine occlusion directly in the mouth or indirectly with dental analysis casts for incisor, canine, and molar relationship on both sides Examine for dental midline and the cusp fossa relationship bilaterally

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Selection of Crown
retrieved_docs.content: Loss of mesiodistal dimension resulting from space loss distal to the mandibular primary first molar complicates selecting an appropriate size crown (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown in Primary First Molar). In such cases, measuring the dimension of the contralateral tooth is advised. [12] A correctly fitting crown should snap into place at try-in. [13] Other factors should be considered when selecting the crown: occlusal anatomy, primate spaces, and crown height. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Wits Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: The Wits analysis provides an alternative approach to evaluating the anteroposterior skeletal pattern without relying on the cranial base. This method involves drawing perpendicular lines from points A and B to the occlusal plane, defined as the line joining the cusps of the posterior teeth. [14] The intersections of the perpendiculars from points A and B with the occlusal plane define points AO and BO, respectively. [14] The distance between AO and BO is then measured. For a class I skeletal pattern, BO is typically 1 mm (±1.9 mm) anterior to AO in males, while in females, BO and AO are generally equal (±1.77 mm). [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Adjacent Stainless Steel Crown With Arch Length Loss/Space Loss
retrieved_docs.content: Reduction of the crown's mesiodistal dimension is frequently required, particularly when mesial drift (often caused by caries) has resulted in arch length loss. Flattening the crown's mesial and distal contact areas using Adam pliers results in a moderate reduction in the mesiodistal dimension. A contralateral upper tooth crown with a smaller size can be used if there is mesial drift in the lower arch. Another form of modification is vertical slicing and spot-welding, an additional segment of a stainless steel band. This is done to increase the perimeter or extend the length. However, the efficacy of the latter modifications remains largely untested. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the transverse dimension, normal occlusion is when the palatine cusps of the upper molars and premolars occlude in the fossa of the lower molars and premolars. In the anteroposterior plane, the upper incisors occlude on the labial aspects of the lower incisors.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Vertical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The position of mandibular incisors is further evaluated by the angle formed between the long axis of the tooth and the mandibular plane, which extends from gonion to gnathion. [10] The normal mandibular incisor to mandibular plane angle (Go-Gn) is 87 degrees. An increased mandibular incisor to mandibular plane angle indicates that the incisors are proclined, whereas a decreased value indicates the incisors are retroclined. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Advantages and Disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: Traditional access cavity This method involves the complete removal of the pulp chamber roof and direct entry to the root canal. Advantages: Provides unobstructed access and visibility for instrumentation. Disadvantages: Greater loss of tooth structure, increased risk of weakening the tooth, and increased risk of fracture. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: One such radiograph unique to orthodontics is the cephalometric projection. The cephalometric projections capture a lateral image of the face whereby relationships between the skull and the jaws can be made. These projections can additionally help orthodontists predict the dental age of a patient and growth potential and highlight sagittal discrepancies, all of which can aid treatment planning. [21]

retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester, is mapped to short cervical length in pregnancy, indicating that the condition of cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is understood as a short cervical length—a key obstetric measurement used to evaluate the risk of preterm birth and guide patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431

retrieved_docs.content: labor and delivery. Recognizing these interrelated conditions enables clinicians to anticipate potential delivery challenges, plan appropriate interventions (such as external cephalic version, cervical assessment, or operative delivery), and optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical os patulous is synonymous with قصور عنق الرحم, a condition characterized by cervical insufficiency. Individuals classified as high risk are clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم, indicating that patients with this risk profile are more likely to exhibit or develop cervical os patulous and its related complications. Recognizing the equivalence between cervical os patulous and قصور عنق الرحم, as well as its link to high‑risk status, is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2064
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is classified as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that affect the management of the mother. Within this broader category, cervical shortening, third trimester is identified as a specific manifestation of cervical shortening in pregnancy. Recognizing cervical shortening in pregnancy, especially when it occurs in the third trimester, is important for obstetric care because it signals potential adverse outcomes and guides clinical decisions during both the antenatal and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change occurring in the second trimester is considered a sign of early or threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in the third trimester is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change is recognized as a sign of potential onset of labor before term.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Induction by cervical dilation and other procedures to assist delivery are closely related interventions used to facilitate childbirth, and they may be considered synonymous in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: The cervical canal structure is anatomically situated within the structure of the internal os, with the internal os serving as the parent structure that encompasses the cervical canal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: aligning it with other cervical structural abnormalities that can affect pregnancy outcomes. **Cervical shortening in pregnancy** is placed under “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” and also belongs to the broader class of **abnormalities of cervix (obstetric)**. It is subdivided into trimester‑specific forms—**Cervical shortening, second trimester**, **Cervical shortening, third trimester**, and **Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester**—each of which is mapped to **Early or threatened labor**, indicating that a shortened cervical length in any trimester serves as a clinical marker for premature or threatened delivery. The unspecified‑trimester form is additionally linked to the concept **Short cervical length in pregnancy**, reinforcing its role as a measurable obstetric parameter.
